title: Images vs Containers
description:
## Many From One

You run the following two commands:

```
docker run -d --name web1 nginx:alpine
docker run -d --name web2 nginx:alpine
```

Which statement best describes what now exists?

options.text: Two copies of the `nginx:alpine` image, each with one container
One `nginx:alpine` image and two independent containers, each with its own writable layer on top of the shared read-only image layers
One image and one container; the second command is ignored because the image is already running
Two containers that share a single writable filesystem

explanation: An image is **read-only** and is stored once. Each `docker run` creates a new container that adds a thin **writable layer** on top of the image's shared read-only layers (copy-on-write). So `web1` and `web2` are isolated — writing a file in one does not affect the other or the underlying image — yet they don't duplicate the image's storage.

hints.title: List images and containers
hints.body: Compare the number of images to the number of containers to see the one-to-many relationship.
